coredata.dc:title: Glycoprotein-based bioimaging of HeLa cancer cells by folate receptor and folate decorated graphene quantum dots
coredata.dc:creator: Soleymani, Jafar
Azizi, Sajjad
Abbaspour-Ravasjani, Soheil
Hasanzadeh, Mohammad
Hossein Somi, Mohammad
Jouyban, Abolghasem
coredata.dc:description: Folate receptor (FR, 38 kDa) is a glycosylphosphatidylinositol glycoprotein which overexpressed on the surface of various cancer cells and has been utilized for wide applications. Folic acid (FA) functionalized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots wrapped terbium (III) tungstate nanoparticles (Tb2(WO4)3NPs@N-GQDs-FA or Tb-W@GQDs-FA) as a smart nanocomposite was synthesized and used for specific imaging of folate receptor (FR) positive HeLa cancer cells. The mechanism of action is based on the attachment of FA decorated nanomaterials to the FR on the surface of cancer cells. This bioimaging probe emits fluorescence emission at 450 nm upon excitation on 345 nm. Flow cytometry and fluorescence microscopy techniques were applied to evaluate the efficiency of uptake of Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the cancer cells. Also, the selective attachment of Tb-W@GQDs-FA particles towards FR-positive HeLa cancer cells was verified by incubation of Tb-W@GQDs-FA with FR-negative normal cells. The cellular toxicity of the produced Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite was evaluated and results approved its biocompatible nature at the concentration of about 400 µg/mL. In conclusion, Tb-W@GQDs-FA could be effectively applied for noninvasive bio-imaging of HeLa cancer cells.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Folate bio-receptor
Glycoprotein
Cervical cancer
Bioimaging
Advanced nanomaterial

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Cell culture and cell viability assay
Evaluation of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA for targeting FR-positive cancer cells
Bioimaging of HeLa cancer cells via Tb-W@GQDs-FA

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial supports from Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) (registration no: 97010508).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM of Tb2(WO4)3, (b) Tb-W@GQDs-FA, (c, d) FESEM images, and (e) graphical structure of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite. (Image (a) adapted from Ref. [27]).
(a) Absorption and (b) fluorescence spectra of Tb-W@GQDs-FA, Tb-W@GQDs and FA, and (c) emitted fluorescence under 400 nm at various concentrations (A) 1.5, (B) 3, (C) 6, (D) 12, and (E) 30 µg/mL).

Possible cell apoptosis induced by Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanoparticles in HeLa cancer cells. (a) Annexin V/PI staining in Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanoparticles over 48 h and (b) fluorescent images of treated HeLa cells stained with DAPI.
Florescence microscopy images of the uptake of Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanomaterials at various concentrations (A) and time of incubation (B). (Scale bar: 20 µm).
(a) Synthesis of Tb-W@GQDs-FA according EDC/NHS approach, and (b) demonstration of binding of FA-functionalized Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the FR-positive cancer cells.

head.ce:title: Glycoprotein-based bioimaging of HeLa cancer cells by folate receptor and folate decorated graphene quantum dots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Liver and Gastrointestinal Diseases Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pharmaceutical Analysis Research Center and Faculty of Pharmacy, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Drug Applied Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Immunology Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Faculty of Pharmacy, Near East University, PO BOX: 99138 Nicosia, North Cyprus, Mersin 10, Turkey
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Food and Drug Safety Research Center, Tabriz University of Medical Sciences, Tabriz, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Folate receptor (FR, 38 kDa) is a glycosylphosphatidylinositol glycoprotein which overexpressed on the surface of various cancer cells and has been utilized for wide applications. Folic acid (FA) functionalized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots wrapped terbium (III) tungstate nanoparticles (Tb2(WO4)3NPs@N-GQDs-FA or Tb-W@GQDs-FA) as a smart nanocomposite was synthesized and used for specific imaging of folate receptor (FR) positive HeLa cancer cells. The mechanism of action is based on the attachment of FA decorated nanomaterials to the FR on the surface of cancer cells. This bioimaging probe emits fluorescence emission at 450 nm upon excitation on 345 nm. Flow cytometry and fluorescence microscopy techniques were applied to evaluate the efficiency of uptake of Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the cancer cells. Also, the selective attachment of Tb-W@GQDs-FA particles towards FR-positive HeLa cancer cells was verified by incubation of Tb-W@GQDs-FA with FR-negative normal cells. The cellular toxicity of the produced Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite was evaluated and results approved its biocompatible nature at the concentration of about 400 µg/mL. In conclusion, Tb-W@GQDs-FA could be effectively applied for noninvasive bio-imaging of HeLa cancer cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Cancer has remained one of the main problem of human health worldwide and its early stage diagnosis is the most effective way to decrease its mortality [1,2]. Early detection of diseases holds significant health, emotional and financial benefits. Currently, immunosensors and immunoassays are largely utilized for the detection of various cancers which mainly based on the biomarkers of each cancer [3–7]. Although the assay based techniques provide the detection of cancers but they usually are more expensive, time-consuming with sophisticated protocols [8,9]. So, introduction of fast and accurate methods for cancer detection is of great importance [10,11].

Among various cancer therapy methods, removing of the solid tumor by surgery is the best way. However, success of surgery relies on the entire remove of solid tumor which mainly depends on the precise determination of the location of the solid tumor. Monitoring of the precise place of tumors is vital for an effective solid tumor surgery wherein precise bioimaging method is required. Optical spectroscopic methods such as photoacoustic (PA), X-ray, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), positron emission tomography (PET), etc. are the dominate techniques in bioimaging of cancer solid tumors [5,12,13] However, these bioimaging devices have some limitations including complex preparing steps, requiring harmful radioactive materials, low speed, and need to very skilled persons to run the instrument and interpret the obtained results that limit their applications in tumor imaging.

Materials with high emission throughput and low interfering signals in biomedical are especially welcomed in imaging of tumors. Any advancement in the introduction of new optically-active materials can remarkably enhance spatiotemporal resolution and sensitivity of cancer detection approaches. Especially, fluorescence bioimaging approaches has attracted attentions to be as a routine method for monitoring and localization of biomarkers and solid tumors which exploiters advantages such as damage-free bioimaging capabilities [14,15]. Fluorescence imaging visualizes the expressions and biochemical interactions of intracellular particular molecules in real-time.

To locate cancer tumor with high specificity, application of cancer cell surface receptors is one of the most specific chooses which enhance sensitivity along with the specificity of the targeting approach. Folate receptor (FR) is a glycolpolypeptide which is overexpressed on the surface of many cancer cells while its expression on the normal cells is limited and considered as a biomarker of cancer [16]. The surface level of FR on cancer cells is associated with the morbidity of cancers [17]. Among the surface receptors, FR/folic acid (FA) interactions are more specific because of their high dissociation constant (Kd ~ 10 nM) which supports more strong bonds between FA and FR [16]. Many types of nanomaterials have been modified with FA molecules to enhance the specificity of drug delivery, sensing and bioimaging of FA-positive cancer cells [18–23]. Soleymani et al. [24] reviewed most recent application of FA-functionalized materials for cancer cell detection also Sudimack et al. [25] and Fernández et al. [26] summarized the applications of FA/FR interaction in drug-delivery and bioimaging, respectively.

Various advanced materials were attached to FA to be used as smart materials for targeting FR-overexpressed cancer cells. However, fluorescence materials with high quantum yield provide as extraordinary to construct platforms for cancer cells bioimaging. The main limitations for application of these materials are their leaching in biological media and secondary toxicity of their constituents. Despite the limitations, high quantum yield fluorescent materials are important candidate materials to replace with the traditionally materials for bioimaging aims.

Herein we synthesized nitrogen doped graphene quantum dots (N-GQDs) based florescence nanocomposite for bioimaging of HeLa cancer cells. Firstly, Tb-W@GQDs were synthesized and then FA was further attached to the Tb-W@GQDs by EDC/NHS reaction. The stability, biocompatibility and toxicity of the produced Tb-W@GQDs-FA are investigated. From the obtained results, it is obvious that the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite could effectively attach onto the surface of HeLa cells via FR-mediated interactions.

Terbium chloride hexahydrate (TbCl3·6H2O) was purchased from Acros Organics (New Jersey, USA). FA was obtained from Mehr Darou (Tehran, Iran). N-hydroxysuccinimide (NHS, 98.0%), 1-ethyl-3-(3-dimethylaminopropyl) carbodiimide (EDC, 98%), triethanolamine (TEA, 98%), phosphate buffer solution (PBS) and 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were provided from Sigma-Aldrich Co. (Taufkirchen, Germany). Dimethylsulfoxide (DMSO) was provided from Merck (Darmstadt, Germany). Roswell Park Memorial Institute 1640 growth medium (RPMI) powder was provided from Gibco BRL Life Technologies (New York, USA). Fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin-EDTA (25%) and streptomycin/penicillin solution were obtained from Biowest (Nuaillé, France). HEK 293 (normal) and HeLa (cancer) cells were obtained from National Cell Bank of Iran (NCBI) in Tehran (Iran).

Fluorescence emissions were measured with a JASCO FP 750 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan) equipped with a thermostat for temperature control. Fluorescence and transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were captured by a Model Bh2-RFCA fluorescence Olympus microscope (Tokyo, Japan) and CytationTM 5 (Winooski, USA) and Carl Zeiss LEO 906 electron microscope operated at 100 kV (Oberkochen, Germany), respectively. Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectra were recorded by a Shimadzu model FTIR prestige 21 spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscopy (FESEM) and energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) were conducted on FEG-SEM MIRA3 TESCAN (Brno, Czech Republic). Zeta potential and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were used for the determination of surface charge and size distribution of Tb-W@GQDs-FA which carried out by a Malvern particle size analyzer (Malvern, UK). Flow cytometry studies were performed by FACScalibur flow-cytometer, Becton Dickinson Immunocytometry Systems (California, USA) and MACS Quant 10; Miltenyi Biotech GmbH (Gladbach, Germany). MTT assay test was implemented at 570 nm by a microplate reader Awareness Technology (Florida, USA). Annexin V/propidium iodide (PI) apoptosis detection kit was purchased from Exbio (Vestec, Czech Republic).

Terbium (III) tungstate nanoparticles were synthesized according to an optimized process which was previously reported by Rahimi-Nasrabadi et al. [27]. Generally, about 0.05 gr of TbCl3·6H2O was added over to the tungsten ion solution (0.01 M) and then stirred for about 12 hrs. The white solid was filtered and rinsed many times with absolute ethanol and water. Finally, as prepared participated was dried in an oven at 75 °C for about 4 hrs.

Tb-W@GQDs was synthesized according to a recently published paper by Miao et al. [28] with several modifications. 1 gr of citric acid and 0.4 mL of ethylenediamine were added into 15 mL water and stirred to dissolve. Then, 50 mg of as-prepared Tb2(WO4)3 NPs was added to the mixture. Next, the aqueous mixture was transferred into a Teflon-lined stainless hydrothermal synthesis autoclave and allowed to be heated up to 180 °C and kept for about 8 hrs. Afterwards, the mixture was cooled to room temperature. Finally, as-synthesized nanomaterials were centrifuged (8.000 rpm for 20 min) to separate solid particles. The obtained Tb-W@GQDs was dialyzed possible unreacted materials.

FA-NHS compounds were prepared according to the EDC/NHS reaction. Briefly, 120 mg of FA, 24 mg of NHS and 20 mg of EDC were dissolved in about 12 mL of DMSO and then 150 μL of TEA was added to the mixture and agitated at room temperature for about 18 hrs. Subsequently, TEA molecules were evaporated using a vacuum. Finally, the produced FA-NHS was kept in −4 °C for the next uses.

20 mL of Tb-W@GQDs solution was added to the NHS-FA solution and stirred at room temperature for about 3 hrs. Finally, Tb-W@GQDs-FA black-brown solution was centrifuged and dialyzed (3000 Da) towards deionized-waste for at least 3 days. As-prepared Tb-W@GQDs-FA is stable at −4 °C for at least two months. Scheme 1 a represents the functionalization of Tb-W@GQDs with FA molecules.

Carboxylic groups of FA molecules are activated according to the EDC/NHS (as reported in 2.3.3. section). EDC activates COOH groups and produces an O-acylisourea intermediate that ready reacts with primary amines to produce an amide bond. The produced intermediate has limited stability and could be hydrolysis in aqueous solution to regenerate carboxyls groups. Hence, NHS molecules are added to form an NHS ester that is relatively more stable than the O-acylisourea and permits for conjugation to primary amines at neutral pH. Amine groups of GQDs can freely attach to the activated FA molecules to produce Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell culture and cell viability assay
ce:section.ce:para: HeLa cancer cells were seeded into 25-mL flasks with RPMI-1640 medium with 10% FBS, and 1% penicillin/streptomycin and then incubated in a humidified atmosphere (37 °C and 5% CO2) for about three days to reach desired confluency of cells. Next, the supernatant of flasks was discarded and washed with buffer solution (pH = 7.4). After addition of trypsin-EDTA, the flasks were incubated for about 5 min to detach the HeLa cells from the flask surface. Then, cell suspensions were centrifuged at 1500 rpm for about 5 min to remove unreacted trypsin-EDTA. Finally, the cells were introduced in refresh RPMI media to count with a haemocytometer (0.0025 mm2). The same procedure was used for culture of HEK 293 normal cells.

Toxicity of Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite to HeLa cells was studied by MTT assay. Firstly, about 1.0 × 104 of HeLa cancer cells were added into the wells of a ninety-six-well microplates and incubated for 24 hrs. Next, various concentrations of Tb-W@GQDs-FA were added to the wells and incubated for desired times (24, 48 and 72 hrs). Then, MTT reagent with concentration of 3.0 mg/mL was added and incubated for another 4 hrs. Afterwards, the unreacted media was removed and about 200 μL of DMSO was added and then incubated for 0.5 hr. Finally, the absorbance values were recorded at 570 nm.

Smart and specific attachment of the produced Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the FR-overexpressed cancer cells was checked upon uptake study. Both imaging and flow cytometry studies were applied to determine uptake degree. To quantitatively measure uptake, HeLa cells were cultured in six-well textured-culture plate till reach a confluency of 5 × 105 cells/mL. Then, various concentrations of Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite were added to the well and subsequently incubated different times. Next, unconsumed media of wells were removed and rinsed with buffer solution. Finally, the uptake of Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the HeLa cells were qualitatively recorded by a fluorescence microscope. To quantitatively measure the uptake percent, flow cytometry analysis was utilized according the same approach reported for fluorescence imaging. However, in the final step, Tb-W@GQDs-FA-assembled cancer cells were detached by trypsin and re-suspended in buffer solution and analyzed by the flow cytometry technique.

DAPI (4′,6-diamidino-2-phenylindole) staining assay is a method aimed to show cytotoxicity by chromatin fragmentation. About 5 × 105 HeLa cells were seeded into the six‐well textured plates which equipped with slips. After 2–3 days, various concentrations of Tb-W@GQDs-FA were added to the wells and incubated for 48 hrs. Then, a 5% solution of formaldehyde was added to the cells to fix the cells upon 3 hrs and then Triton X‐100 was added to permeabilize the HeLa cells. Next, DAPI (0.1%) was added to stain the cells for 10 min and finally fluorescence imagines were captured.

About 5 × 105 HeLa cells were seeded to each well of a six-textured plate and incubated overnight in a RPMI media containing 400 µg/mL of Tb-W@GQDs-FA at 37 °C for 48 hrs. Then, the cells were separated using trypsin and washed with PBS. The detached cell was resuspended in binding buffer media. Finally, the HeLa cells were stained by annexin V/propidium iodide (PI) apoptosis detection kit according to the kit guidelines. Then, the apoptosis rate was determined flow cytometry and the obtained results were analyzed by FlowJo software (CA, USA).

Various methods such as FESEM, TEM, DLS, FTIR, and zeta potential techniques were used for the characterization of the synthesis of nanoparticles. Fig. 1 shows the TEM (Fig. 1a and b) and SEM (Fig. 1c and d) of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite. As could be seen, Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite shows the quasi-spherical form in which Tb2(WO4)3 is the core section and the FA-functionalized N-GQDs covered its surface with a final size of about 100 nm. Initial size of Tb2(WO4)3 was reported around 45 nm, however, after functionalization with FA and N-GQDs, a meaningful increase in the size of Tb2(WO4)3 is observed. The FESEM images approve the morphology of the nanocomposite and confirm the attachment of N-GQDs on the surface of Tb-W@GQDs-FA. Fig. 1e represents graphical structure of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite, according to the TEM and SEM images. Besides the well-matched SEM and TEM images, EDX results confirmed that the terbium and tungsten oxide bimetallic core are inserted to the nanocomposite and N-GQDs are grown on the Tb2(WO4)3 nanocomposite surface (Fig. 1S). As could be seen, about 1.82 and 3.41 wt% of the produced nanocomposite are composed of Tb and W metals, respectively. After addition of N-GQDs and FA components to the Tb2(WO4)3 NPs, a shape spectrum of carbon and nitrogen are appeared, indicating the anchoring of then on the surface of Tb2(WO4)3NPs [27].

The hydrodynamic size of Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite was checked by DLS to determine wet size of the nanocomposite. Results showed that its hydrodynamic size is about 109 nm which already confirmed by TEM images (Fig. 2 S (a).

Zeta potential and FTIR techniques were applied to determine the surface charge and functional groups of Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite, respectively. From zeta potential profile in Fig. 2S (b), it is obvious that the surface charge is in negative (-21.5 mV) form which mainly as result of attachment of FA molecules (with zeta-potential of −31.6 mV) on amine ends of N-GQDs nanosheets. Compare to the other FA functionalized nanomaterials such as N-GQDs-FA, the zeta potential value is more negative which may be caused by a metallic core of the nanocomposite [22].

The best technique to chemically approve anchoring of FA to the surface of Tb-W@GQDs is FTIR in which the functional groups are approved. As shown in Fig. 3 S, the successful synthesis and functionalization of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite are approved according to the following characteristic spectra. Spectra located at 725 cm−1 and 990 cm−1 are attributed to the O–W–O and W–O stretching vibrations. The Tb-specific spectrum is usually located at 450 cm−1 which is related to the stretching of Tb-O bonds, but due to the low weight percent of terbium in Tb-W@GQDs-FA, this spectrum was not realized [29]. Spectrum at 1500 cm−1 is related to amide bonds between carboxyl groups of FA and amine groups of Tb-W@GQDs-NH2 nanocomposite molecules. The recent peak is the characteristic peak of the attachment of FA to the amine groups of components through its COOH functional groups [30].

Upon EDC/NHS reaction, FA molecules are easily attached to the amine groups of counter molecules by γ-carboxyl group. The primary amine groups of N-GQDs on the Tb-W@GQDs nanocomposite make them to be combined with the γ-COOH groups of FA molecules. The photophysical properties of the Tb-W@GQDs nanocomposite were investigated before and after the addition of FA molecules. As shown in Fig. 2 (a), the characteristic spectra of FA molecules were obviously observed at around 285 nm and 350 nm. N-GQDs nanoparticles show two spectra at 230 nm and 330 nm which could be ascribed to the π–π* electron transition of C = C sp2 and the excited energy of surface of-N-GQDs, respectively, however, N-GQDs specific spectrum was overlapped by FA spectrum at 330 nm [31]. After the addition of FA molecules to the Tb-W@GQDs nanocomposite, minor red-shift was occurred, approving the successful attachment of FA molecules onto the primary amines of Tb-W@GQDs nanocomposite. As above explained, zeta-potential, FTIR, TEM and FESEM images confirmed the proper functionalization Tb-W@GQDs with FA.

The influence of surface modification of Tb-W@GQDs with FA molecules was investigated (Fig. 2 (b) and (c)). After attachment of FA molecules, only minor change was occurred in the fluorescence of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite. The functionalization of nanoparticles with folate molecules can logically bring the fluorescence spectrum of nanoparticles closer to the folate spectrum.

The effects of ionic strength, room temperature and refrigerator storage, pH and UV exposure time on the Tb-W@GQDs-FA fluorescent intensity were critically investigated (Fig. 4 S). The fluorescence intensity of Tb-W@GQDs-FA was about constant at pH values from 5 and 10. In addition, the florescence intensity of the probe did not significantly upon changing of the ionic strength of the solution by NaCl. Obtained results revealed that Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite is stable against UV light and photobleaching did not happen during UV-exposure. Stability tested showed that optical intensity of Tb-W@GQDs-FA was not significantly changed upon several times room temperature and refrigerator storages. These results confirmed that the Tb-W@GQDs-FA had excellent photo and storage stabilities.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Evaluation of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA for targeting FR-positive cancer cells

ce:section.ce:para: Cell cytotoxicity of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA was evaluated by MTT assay using HeLa and HEK 293 cells. Fig. 5S presents cell viability of various concentrations of Tb-W@GQDs-FA from 1.0 to 400 µg/mL for at least 48 hrs which implicated that about 80 % of both cells were alive in all concentration ranges. Thus, Tb-W@GQDs-FA could be utilized as biocompatible nanocomposite for bioimaging of cancer cells.

Quantitative apoptosis of HeLa cells during incubation with Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite was determined by staining with annexin V/PI. To do, HeLa cells were incubated with the nanocomposite for 48 hrs. In this study, blank cells were only stained with annexin nor the nanocomposite and regarded as the control group. As Fig. 3 shows, there is no meaningful different between the control negative and nanocomposite treated groups, confirming the low toxicity of the nanocomposite. Therefore, Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanoparticles have biocompatible nature where results approve again the MTT assay results.

To further investigate the biocompatibility of the nanocomposite, DAPI staining assay was utilized. DAPI assay was used to qualitatively check the effect of the nanocomposite on the nucleus of the cells. From Fig. 3, it is obvious that the morphology of the HeLa cells’ nucleus was not significantly affected by Tb-W@GQDs-FA during incubation with 400 µgr/mL of the nanocomposite and minimum deformation and fragmentation on the nucleus of the cells were occurred. The obtained DAPI and annexin assays results are in accordance with the MTT assay results, approving the biocompatible nature of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Bioimaging of HeLa cancer cells via Tb-W@GQDs-FA
ce:section.ce:para: The uptake of Tb-W@GQDs-FA into HeLa cells was studied. It is supposed that FR-positive HeLa cells can endocytosis the Tb-W@GQDs-FA through FRs. The specific attachment can happen between FR-overexpressed cancer cells and FA moiety of Tb-W@GQDs-FA since FA of the nanocomposite possesses comparable affinity to the surface FRs of cancer cells as free FAs. The mechanism of the attachment of Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the FR-positive cells is demonstrated in Scheme 1b.

As Fig. 4 shows, a bright red fluorescence is emitted by Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite attached on the surface of HeLa cells after the incubation for 2 hrs. The obtained results apparently indicate that the brightness of fluorescence images is proportionally associated to the FR-density on the cancer cell surface, and providing the discernable fluorescent images.

From the images it is clear that only cytoplasm and membrane of the cells were attached to the Tb-W@GQDs-FA and nucleus were not significantly colored by Tb-W@GQDs-FA, demonstrating that Tb-W@GQDs-FA can only attach to the membrane and penetrate into the cell cytoplasm more than the cell nucleus. These results are accordance with the fact that FRs largely are available in the membrane of FR-overexpressed cancer cells.

Various factors can affect the brightness of the images such as free concentration of FA, incubation time and concentration of the nanoparticles which applied for bio imaging. Remove of free FA molecules after the synthesis are very importance because of the fact that FRs of HeLa cells were earlier bonded with free FA molecules and thus Tb-W@GQDs-FA are prevented to attach to the cancer cells, resulting in low resolution images.

As shown in Fig. 3, concentration of the nanocomposite and incubation time have the main effect on the amount of the assembling of Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the surface of HeLa cells. Upon increase on the concentration of the nanocomposite, the amount of FA is increased and thus resulted in enhancement of FA and FR bonds. However, high concentration of nanocomposite may be cause low attachment due to the possible repulsive interaction among the nanoparticles (Fig. 4A).

Fig. 4B demonstrates the effect of incubation time on the efficiency of the interaction between FRs of cancer cells and FA of the nanocomposite. As seen, the uptake amount reached on its maximum level after about 3 hrs incubation, approving the effective attachment of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the HeLa cancer cells.

Fig. 6S provides quantitative analysis the amount of the uptake of Tb-W@GQDs-FA to the FR-positive HeLa cells. As could be seen, bioimaging results are approved by flow cytometry results. To check the applicability of the produced nanocomposite to other FR-positive cancer cells, the uptake of Tb-W@GQDs-FA was checked to the MCF 7 breast cancer cells. As presented in Fig. 7S, Tb-W@GQDs-FA can readily attach to the cell membrane of the MCF 7cancer cells. However, due to the high density of FR on the surface of HeLa cancer cells, the cellular uptake of Tb-W@GQDs-FA is high to the HeLa cells rather than MCF 7 cells [32].

The differentiation ability of Tb-W@GQDs-FA was evaluated with HEK 293 and HeLa cells. HEK 293 cells are epithelial normal cell with low level of FR expression [33]. To check the specificity of the produced nanocomposite, HEK 293 normal cells were incubated with the Tb-W@GQDs-FA. Fluorescence images of the HEK 293 cell showed that the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite is able to differentiate HEK 293 FR-negative cells from FR-positive HeLa cells, demonstrating that the nanocomposite could specifically detect cancer cells from normal cells.

Because of the high affinity of FA molecules to FR of cell membrane, the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite was adhered to FR-overexpressed HeLa cells spontaneously but binding to the HEK 293 cells are restricted. As predicted, incubation of HeLa cells with the Tb-W@GQDs-FA nanocomposite displayed amplified fluorescence than HEK 293 FR-negative cells. HEK 293 normal cells were incubated with Tb-W@GQDs-FA and results exhibited no uptake, indicating the specific targeting of FR-positive HeLa cells (Fig. 8S).

A new FA-targeted fluorescence platform for specific bioimaging and identification of FR-positive HeLa cancer cells was designed. The Tb2(WO4)3NPs attached on the surface of N-GQDs to increase possible sites for attachment of FA molecules and further enhance the amount of bio-conjugation of the Tb-W@GQDs-FA with FR-overexpressed cancer cells. It was confirmed that the FA-modified nanocomposite provides an efficient bioimaging approach of cancer cells by improving the capabilities of the nanoparticles to boost the binding affinities between FA and FR. The fluorescence platform can specifically bind to the FR-overexpressed HeLa cancer cells, and the bright-field and fluorescence images can show the attachment of the nanoparticles in HeLa cancer cells. The produced nanomaterials can offer a differentiation approach for FR-positive and FR-negative cells. The engineering fluorescent based nanocomposite with good biocompatibility and high cellular targeting facility to FR-positive cancer cells have great potential to be utilized as an appropriate bioimaging agents for targeting of the HeLa cancer cells.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors would like to acknowledge the financial supports from Iran National Science Foundation (INSF) (registration no: 97010508).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2021.106732.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Prostate cancer: diagnostic criteria and role of immunohistochemistry

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Magi-Galluzzi, Prostate cancer: diagnostic criteria and role of immunohistochemistry, Mod. Pathol. 31 (2018) S12. https://doi.org/10.1038/modpathol.2017.139.
